volumeTitle: The Little Carthaginian. Pseudolus. The Rope
label: The Rope (Section 4.4.1178 - Section 4.8.1268b)

sections.label: Section 4.4.1178
sections.original: Ecce Gripi scelera . cum istaec res male evenit tibi ,
sections.translation: See the villany of Gripus ; inasmuch , Gripus , as this matter has turned out unfortunately for you , I congratulate you .

sections.label: Section 4.4.1179b
sections.original: Age eamus , mea gnata , ad matrem tuam ,
sections.translation: Come , then , let ’ s go , my daughter , to your mother ,

sections.label: Section 4.4.1180
sections.original: quae ex te poterit argumentis hanc rem magis exquirere ,
sections.translation: who will be better able to enquire of you into this matter from proofs ; who had you more in her hands , and is more thoroughly acquainted with your tokens .

sections.label: Section 4.4.1182
sections.original: Eamus intro ómnes , quando óperam promiscam damus .—
sections.translation: Let ’ s all go hence in-doors , since we are giving our common aid .

sections.label: Section 4.4.1183
sections.original: Sequere me , Ampelisca .—
sections.translation: Follow me , Ampelisca .

sections.label: Section 4.4.1183b
sections.original: Cum te di amant , voluptati est mihi .—
sections.translation: That the Gods favour you , it is a pleasure to me .

sections.label: Section 4.4.1184
sections.original: Sumne ego sceléstus , qui illunc hodie excepi vidulum ?
sections.translation: Am I not a blockhead of a fellow , to have this day fished up that wallet ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.1185
sections.original: aut cum excepi , qui non alicubi in solo abstrusi loco ?
sections.translation: Or , when I had fished it up , not to have hidden it somewhere in a secret spot ? By my troth , I guessed that it would be a troublesome booty for me , because it fell to me in such troublous weather . I ’ faith , I guess that there ’ s plenty of gold and silver there . What is there better for me than to be off hence in-doors and secretly hang myself —

sections.label: Section 4.4.1190
sections.original: saltem tantisper dum abscedat haec a me aegrimonia ?—
sections.translation: at least for a little time , until this vexation passes away from me ?

sections.label: Section 4.5.1191
sections.original: Pro di immortales , quis me est fortunatior ,
sections.translation: O ye immortal Gods , what person is there more fortunate than I , who unexpectedly have discovered my daughter ? Isn ’ t it the fact , that if the Gods will a blessing to befall any person , that longed-for pleasure by some means or other , falls to the lot [*] of the virtuous ?

sections.label: Section 4.5.1195
sections.original: ego hodie neque speravi neque illud credidi :
sections.translation: I this day , a thing that I never hoped for nor yet believed , have unexpectedly discovered my daughter , and I shall bestow her upon a respectable young man of noble family , an Athenian , and my kinsman . For that reason I wish him to be fetched hither to me as soon as possible ,

sections.label: Section 4.5.1200
sections.original: iussique exire huc servom éius , ut ad forum
sections.translation: and I ’ ve requested my servant to come out here , that he may go to the Forum . Still , I ’ m surprised at it that he isn ’ t yet come out . I think I ’ ll go to the door . ( Opens the door , and looks in .) What do I behold ? Embracing her , my wife is clasping my daughter around her neck . Her caressing is really almost too foolish and sickening .

sections.label: Section 4.6.1205
sections.original: Aliquando osculando meliust , uxor , pausam fieri ;
sections.translation: ’ Twere better , wife , for an end to be made at last of your kissing ; and make all ready that I may perform a sacrifice , when I come in-doors , in honor of the household Gods , inasmuch as they have increased our family . At home I have lambs and swine for sacred use . But why , ladies , are you detaining that Trachalio ? Oh , I see he ’ s coming out of doors , very seasonably .

sections.label: Section 4.6.1210
sections.original: Vbi ubi erit , iam investigabo et mecum ad te adducam simul
sections.translation: Wheresoever he shall be , I ’ ll seek Plesidippus out at once , and bring him together with me to you .

sections.label: Section 4.6.1211b
sections.original: Eloquere ut haec res optigit de filia ;
sections.translation: Tell him how this matter has fallen out about my daughter . Request him to leave other occupations and to come here .

sections.label: Section 4.6.1212b
sections.original: Licet .
sections.translation: Very well [*]

sections.label: Section 4.6.1213
sections.original: Dicito daturum meam illi filiam uxorem .
sections.translation: Tell him that I ’ ll give him my daughter for a wife .

sections.label: Section 4.6.1213b
sections.original: Licet .
sections.translation: Very well .

sections.label: Section 4.6.1214
sections.original: Et patrem eius me novisse et mi esse cognatum .
sections.translation: And that I knew his father , and that he is a relation of my own .

sections.label: Section 4.6.1214b
sections.original: Licet .
sections.translation: Very well .

sections.label: Section 4.6.1215
sections.original: Sed propera .
sections.translation: But do make haste .

sections.label: Section 4.6.1215b
sections.original: Licet .
sections.translation: Very well .

sections.label: Section 4.6.1215c
sections.original: Iam hic fac sit , cena ut curetur .
sections.translation: Take care and let a dinner be prepared here at once .

sections.label: Section 4.6.1215d
sections.original: Licet .
sections.translation: Very well .

sections.label: Section 4.6.1216
sections.original: Omnian licet ?
sections.translation: What , all very well ?

sections.label: Section 4.6.1216b
sections.original: Licet . sed scin quid est quod te volo ?
sections.translation: Very well . But do you know what it is I want of you ? That you ’ ll remember what you promised , that this day I ’ m to be free .

sections.label: Section 4.6.1217b
sections.original: Licet .
sections.translation: Very well [*] .

sections.label: Section 4.6.1218
sections.original: Fac ut exores Plesidippum , ut me manu emittat .
sections.translation: Take care and entreat Plesidippus to give me my freedom .

sections.label: Section 4.6.1218b
sections.original: Licet .
sections.translation: Very well .

sections.label: Section 4.6.1219
sections.original: Et tua filiá facito oret : facile exorabit .
sections.translation: And let your daughter request it ; she ’ ll easily prevail .

sections.label: Section 4.6.1219b
sections.original: Licet .
sections.translation: Very well .

sections.label: Section 4.6.1220
sections.original: Atque ut mi Ampelisca nubat , ubi ego sim liber .
sections.translation: And that Ampelisca may marry me , when I ’ m a free man .

sections.label: Section 4.6.1220b
sections.original: Licet .
sections.translation: Very well .

sections.label: Section 4.6.1221
sections.original: Atque ut gratum mi beneficium factis experiar .
sections.translation: And that I may experience a pleasing return to myself in kindness for my actions .

sections.label: Section 4.6.1221b
sections.original: Licet .
sections.translation: Very well .

sections.label: Section 4.6.1222
sections.original: Omnian licet ?
sections.translation: What , all very well ?

sections.label: Section 4.6.1222b
sections.original: Licet : tibi rursum refero gratiam .
sections.translation: Very well . Again I return you thanks . But do you make haste to proceed to the city forthwith , and betake yourself hither again .

sections.label: Section 4.6.1223b
sections.original: Licet .
sections.translation: Very well . I ’ ll be here directly . In the meanwhile , do you make the other preparations that are necessary .

sections.label: Section 4.6.1224b
sections.original: Licet .
sections.translation: Very well —

sections.label: Section 4.6.1225
sections.original: Hercules istum infelicet cum sua licentia ;
sections.translation: may Hercules ill befriend him with his very-welling [*] ; he has so stuffed my ears with it . Whatever it was I said , very well was the answer .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1227
sections.original: Quam mox licet te compellare , Daemones ?
sections.translation: How soon may I have a word with you , Daemones ?

sections.label: Section 4.7.1228
sections.original: Quid est negoti , Gripe ?
sections.translation: What ’ s your business , Gripus ?

sections.label: Section 4.7.1228b
sections.original: De illo vidulo :
sections.translation: Touching that wallet , if you are wise , be wise ; keep what goods the Gods provide you .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1230
sections.original: Aequom videtur tibi , ut ego , alienum quod est ,
sections.translation: Does it seem right to you , that , what belongs to another I should assert to be my own ?

sections.label: Section 4.7.1231b
sections.original: Quodne ego inveni in mari ?
sections.translation: What , not a thing that I found in the sea ?

sections.label: Section 4.7.1232
sections.original: Tanto illi melius optigit qui perdidit ;
sections.translation: So much the better does it happen for him who lost it ; none the more is it necessary that it should be your wallet .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1234
sections.original: Isto tu pauper es , quom nimis sancte pius .
sections.translation: For this reason are you poor because you are too scrupulously righteous .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1235
sections.original: O Gripe , Gripe , in aetate hominum plurimae
sections.translation: O Gripus , Gripus , in the life of man very many traps there are , in what they are deceived by guile . And , by my troth , full often is a bait placed in them , which bait if any greedy person greedily snaps at , through his own greediness he is caught in the trap .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1240
sections.original: ille quí consulte , docte atque astute cavet ,
sections.translation: He who prudently , skilfully , and warily , takes precaution , full long he may enjoy that which is honestly acquired . This booty seems to me [*] to be about to be made a booty of by me , that it may go hence with a greater blessing than it first came . What , ought I to conceal what I know was brought to me as belonging to another ?

sections.label: Section 4.7.1245
sections.original: celem ? minime istuc faciet noster Daemones .
sections.translation: By no means will my friend Daemones do that . ’ Tis ever most becoming for prudent men to be on their guard against this , that they be not themselves confederates with their servants in evil-doing . Except only when I ’ m gaming , I don ’ t care for any gain .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1249
sections.original: Spectavi ego pridem comicos ad istúnc modum
sections.translation: At times , I ’ ve seen the Comedians , when acting , in this fashion

sections.label: Section 4.7.1250
sections.original: sapienter dicta dicere atque eis plaudier ,
sections.translation: repeat sayings in a wise manner , and be applauded for them , when they pointed out this prudent conduct to the public . But when each person went thence his own way home , there wasn ’ t one after the fashion which they had recommended .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1254
sections.original: Abi intro , ne molestu'smolestus es , linguae tempera .
sections.translation: Go in-doors , don ’ t be troublesome , moderate your tongue . I ’ m going to give you nothing , don ’ t you deceive yourself .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1256
sections.original: At ego deos quaeso , ut quidquid ín illo vidulost ,
sections.translation: Then I pray the Gods that whatever ’ s in that wallet , whether it ’ s gold , or whether silver , it may all become ashes .

sections.label: Section 4.7.1258
sections.original: Illuc est quod nos nequam servis utimur .
sections.translation: This is the reason why we have bad servants . For this master , if he had combined with any servant ,

sections.label: Section 4.7.1260
sections.original: et ipsum sese et illum furti adstringeret ;
sections.translation: would have made both himself and the other guilty of a theft . While he was thinking that he himself had made a capture , in the meantime he himself would have been made a capture : capture would have led to capture . Now will I go in-doors from here and sacrifice ; after that , I ’ ll at once order the dinner to be cooked for us .

sections.label: Section 4.8.1265
sections.original: Íterum mihi istaec omnia itera , mi anime , mi Trachalio ,
sections.translation: Tell me all these things over again my life , my Trachalio , my freed-man , my patron , aye rather , my father ; has Palaestra found her father and mother ?

sections.label: Section 4.8.1267b
sections.original: Repperit .
sections.translation: She has found them .

sections.label: Section 4.8.1268
sections.original: Et popularis est ?
sections.translation: And is she my countrywoman ?

sections.label: Section 4.8.1268b
sections.original: Opino .
sections.translation: So I think .
